summary:
Constantine the Great's conversion to Christianity (c. 312 AD) was arguably the single most consequential personal religious experience in world history — a military commander's vision before a crucial battle that set in motion the Christianization of the Roman Empire and, through it, of the entire Western world. The traditional account holds that before the Battle of Milvian Bridge (312 AD) against his rival Maxentius, Constantine saw a vision of the Christian chi-rho symbol (the first two letters of 'Christ' in Greek) in the sky, with the words 'In this sign, conquer.' He had his soldiers paint the symbol on their shields; they won the battle.

The sincerity and nature of Constantine's conversion is hotly debated by historians. He issued the [[event:2028|Edict of Milan]] in 313 AD legalizing Christianity (and all religions), convened the [[event:2029|Council of Nicaea]] in 325 AD, built Christian basilicas across the empire, patronized the church lavishly, and was baptized on his deathbed in 337 AD. Whether this represented genuine personal faith or calculated political strategy — or both — is impossible to determine with certainty. What is certain is the effect: Christianity went from persecuted minority religion to the most favored and rapidly growing faith in the empire within a generation, culminating in Theodosius I making it the sole official state religion in 380 AD. Constantine's conversion transformed Christianity from a marginal sect into the dominant religion of Western civilization.
